[@mantel1959]. Both use every control row and draw no random numbers. Strata are deciles of panel-wide
effect magnitude. An earlier version of this analysis sampled a single 100-row matched background; that
procedure made the p-value a random variable, and 22.4% of seeds returned a non-significant result. It
is superseded.

Panel-wide effect magnitude, written $z_{L2}$, is the Euclidean norm of a perturbation's vector of
per-gene z statistics across all `{python} f"{N_PANEL:,}"` measured genes. Matching on $z_{L2}$ means
matching on deciles of this quantity.

Specificity of a shortlist is assessed by drawing 5,000 shortlists of equal size from the same
evidence-passing pool, matched on $z_{L2}$ decile. Monte Carlo probabilities are computed as
$(1 + \#\{{\rm null} \ge {\rm observed}\}) / (1 + n_{\rm draws})$. With 5,000 draws the smallest
attainable value is $1/5001 = 2.0\times10^{-4}$, and a value equal to that floor means no draw reached
the observed statistic. An earlier version of this analysis omitted the correction and reported
$P < 0.0001$, which those draws cannot establish.

**Status of the inference.** The thresholds and modules were fixed before the analyses that used them,
but the axes themselves, the score, and the nomination rule were developed and revised on this dataset.
The probabilities reported here are therefore exploratory. Confirmation would require an untouched
dataset, a held-out donor partition, or a rule specified before the data were seen.

For module-level statements, the unit of analysis is the module, not the perturbation. The
co-inhibitory module is a single fixed nine-gene set; if it covaries with efficacy, every
per-perturbation score shifts together. A per-perturbation Mann-Whitney test over the
`{python} f"{N_RANKABLE:,}"` values therefore has an effective sample size of one module. That test
returned p = `{python} f"{n9['mwu_p_void']:.2g}"` and a false-positive rate of
`{python} f"{n9['mwu_fpr_per_perturbation']:.0%}"` on random modules against a nominal 5%; it is
withdrawn and is not quoted as evidence anywhere in this manuscript. Module-level inference instead
compares the observed statistic against `{python} f"{int(n9['n_modules'])}"` random nine-gene modules
matched on expression and on stimulation induction, holding the perturbation set fixed. The observed
module exceeds every null module, so the resulting probability equals the floor $1/(n+1)$ of that null
and is a bound rather than an estimate.

## External resources

Core-essential (CEGv2) and nonessential (NEGv1) reference gene sets are from @hart2017. Loss-of-function
constraint metrics, LOEUF and pRec, are from gnomAD v4 [@karczewski2020]. Tissue expression breadth is
from the Human Protein Atlas [@uhlen2015]. Target-disease genetic association, tractability and drug
mechanism-of-action annotations are from the Open Targets Platform, release 26.06 [@ochoa2023].

Two CRISPR screens in primary human T cells are used as comparators. @freimer2022 sorted cells on
IL2RA, IL-2 and CTLA-4 protein. @arce2025 sorted on IL-2Rα surface expression across resting effector
T cells, restimulated effector T cells and resting regulatory T cells. Both were analysed with MAGeCK
[@li2014]. These two screens target the **same 1,351-gene library** of transcription factors and
chromatin modifiers (Jaccard index 1.0000, asserted at run time). They are independent in readout and
cell state and are not independent in gene space, and the 472 genes co-tested against our screen are
therefore a transcription-factor and chromatin-modifier panel rather than a random sample of the
perturbed genome.

The CRISPRa and CRISPRi screens of @schmidt2022 are held out. They are used only to validate the
efficacy axis out of sample and are never used as a score, a filter, or a threshold.

## Gene set enrichment

Preranked gene set enrichment analysis [@subramanian2005] was run with `gseapy` [@fang2023] against the
MSigDB Hallmark collection [@liberzon2015], on the 48 h stimulated panel-wide z statistic. The
background is the measured panel by construction. The comparison set is a group of perturbations
matched on panel-wide effect magnitude.

## Nomination rule and its calibration

A nomination rule was selected by its sensitivity on the positive class, that is, the fraction of the
`{python} f"{N_RECOVERED}"` approved-drug targets recovered by the pipeline that the rule would
re-nominate had their drug status not been known. This criterion never inspects which novel gene a rule
promotes. The rule may not read any field derived from approved-drug status; a permutation test asserts
at run time that no such field can change the nominated set.

## Software and reproducibility

Analyses are Python 3.13 with `numpy`, `pandas`, `scipy` and `gseapy`. Every number in this manuscript
is read at render time from a table under `results/tables/`. Two scripts fail with a non-zero exit code
if any quoted value has drifted from its source (@sec-supp-genealogy), and several analysis scripts
exit non-zero if a pre-registered control fails.

# Results

## Perturbations ranked by effector-program suppression {#sec-ranking}

Ranking the `{python} f"{N_RANKABLE:,}"` perturbations by effector-module suppression returns
receptor-proximal signalling genes. Of the twenty highest ranked perturbations, the triage layer
rejects `{python} f"{int(ANY['observed'])}"`. An independent CRISPRi screen with a protein-level
readout [@schmidt2022] confirms that several of these knockdowns do reduce IL-2 production, so they are
effective by an external measure while being refused here.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ranking
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Effector-program suppression and triage outcome. **A.** The twenty perturbations with the highest effector-suppression score, ordered by rank. Bars are coloured by triage outcome. **B.** Rejection counts among those twenty, by axis, against the mean count in 5,000 shortlists of twenty perturbations drawn from the same evidence-passing pool and matched on panel-wide effect magnitude. Rejection on the co-inhibitory axis exceeds the matched expectation; the context-selectivity annotation does not. Error bars are omitted because the matched values are means of the null distribution, and the exact permutation probabilities are printed above each pair."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 4.4

w = window.copy()
w["naive_rank"] = (-w["eff_mean_z"]).rank(ascending=False, method="first")
top20 = w.nsmallest(20, "naive_rank").sort_values("naive_rank")

fig, (axa, axb) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4.4), gridspec_kw={"width_ratios": [1.35, 1]})

axis_style(axa, xgrid=True, ygrid=False)
colours = [PALETTE["blue"] if s else PALETTE["vermillion"] for s in top20["safe"]]
y = np.arange(len(top20))
axa.barh(y, -top20["eff_mean_z"], color=colours, edgecolor="none", height=0.72, zorder=3)
axa.set_yticks(y)
axa.set_yticklabels(top20["gene_name"], fontsize=8, style="italic")
axa.invert_yaxis()
axa.set_xlabel("effector-module suppression (mean $z$, sign inverted)", fontsize=9)
axa.legend(handles=[mpl.patches.Patch(facecolor=PALETTE["vermillion"], label="rejected"),
                    mpl.patches.Patch(facecolor=PALETTE["blue"], label="passes triage")],
           loc="lower right", frameon=False, fontsize=8)
panel_label(axa, "A")

axis_style(axb)
labels = ["co-inhibitory\nmodule", "context selectivity\n(annotation)"]
obs = [int(TOL["observed"]), int(SEL["observed"])]
exp = [float(TOL["matched_mean"]), float(SEL["matched_mean"])]
pvals = [float(TOL["p_matched_ge_observed"]), float(SEL["p_matched_ge_observed"])]
x = np.arange(2)
axb.bar(x - 0.19, obs, width=0.36, color=PALETTE["vermillion"], zorder=3, label="observed, naive top 20")
axb.bar(x + 0.19, exp, width=0.36, color=PALETTE["grey"], zorder=3, label="magnitude-matched mean")
for i, (o, e, p) in enumerate(zip(obs, exp, pvals)):
    txt = "P < 0.0001" if p < 1e-4 else f"P = {p:.2f}"
    axb.text(i, max(o, e) + 0.9, txt, ha="center", fontsize=8.5, color=PALETTE["ink"])
axb.set_xticks(x)
axb.set_xticklabels(labels, fontsize=8.5)
axb.set_ylabel("perturbations rejected (of 20)", fontsize=9)
axb.set_ylim(0, 20)
axb.legend(loc="upper left", frameon=False, fontsize=8)
panel_label(axb, "B")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Specificity of the co-inhibitory axis relative to effect magnitude {#sec-magnitude}

A shortlist of twenty perturbations drawn from the same evidence-passing pool and matched on $z_{L2}$
decile is rejected on the co-inhibitory axis `{python} f"{TOL['matched_mean']:.1f}"` times, against
`{python} f"{int(TOL['observed'])}"` observed. No matched shortlist reached the observed count, so
P = `{python} f"{TOL['p_matched_ge_observed']:.1e}"`, the floor for 5,000 draws (n = 20). The
context-selectivity annotation flags `{python} f"{int(SEL['observed'])}"` of the same twenty against
`{python} f"{SEL['matched_mean']:.1f}"` matched (P = `{python} f"{SEL['p_matched_ge_observed']:.2f}"`).
Selectivity therefore carries no information beyond effect magnitude and is reported as an annotation.

An earlier formulation counted rejections on any axis and reported 18 of 20 against a matched
expectation of 15.3 (P = 0.104). That statement is withdrawn: the aggregate count was substantially
attributable to effect magnitude, and the axis-resolved comparison above supersedes it.

Against a background stratified on panel-wide effect magnitude, using every control row, co-inhibitory
suppression in the naive top 100 exceeds the background by a one-sided van Elteren z of
`{python} f"{PRIMARY['effect']:.2f}"` (p = `{python} f"{PRIMARY['p']:.1e}"`). This is a
perturbation-level association and is not a module-level inference; see @sec-module.

## Module-level inference and the effective unit of analysis {#sec-module}

Both modules are stimulation-induced, so any perturbation that blunts activation reduces both. The test
that separates them holds the perturbation set fixed at the 100 highest-efficacy perturbations and
varies the module. If co-inhibitory suppression reflected activation amplitude alone, a random
nine-gene module matched on expression and on induction would fall equally far.

```{python}
#| label: fig-module-null
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Module-level null. Distribution of the module statistic $T$, the median $z$ of a nine-gene module across the 100 highest-efficacy perturbations, for 200 random modules matched on expression and on stimulation induction (grey). The perturbation set is held fixed, so activation amplitude is constant across the null. The observed value for the activation-induced co-inhibitory module (vermillion) exceeds every matched module. Restricting the null pool to modules of comparable internal co-regulation raises the permutation probability from 0.005 to 0.0196; the latter is the value reported in this manuscript. The positive control, the effector module itself, lies far to the right and is not drawn."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 3.8

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.8))
axis_style(ax)
ax.hist(nulls["T_null"], bins=28, color=PALETTE["grey"], edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.5, zorder=3)
ax.axvline(float(n9["T_real"]), color=PALETTE["vermillion"], linewidth=2, zorder=4)
ax.axvline(float(n9["T_null_max"]), color=PALETTE["ink2"], linewidth=1, linestyle="--", zorder=4)
ax.annotate(f"observed module\n$T$ = {n9['T_real']:.3f}",
            xy=(float(n9["T_real"]), ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.72),
            xytext=(float(n9["T_real"]) - 1.15, ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.86),
            fontsize=8.5, color=PALETTE["ink"],
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color=PALETTE["ink2"], linewidth=0.8))
ax.annotate(f"largest matched null\n{n9['T_null_max']:.3f}",
            xy=(float(n9["T_null_max"]), ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.42),
            xytext=(float(n9["T_null_max"]) - 1.25, ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.52),
            fontsize=8, color=PALETTE["ink2"],
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color=PALETTE["ink2"], linewidth=0.8))
ax.set_xlabel("module statistic $T$ (median $z$ across the 100 highest-efficacy perturbations)", fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel("matched null modules", fontsize=9)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The observed statistic is `{python} f"{n9['T_real']:.3f}"`. The largest of the
`{python} f"{int(n9['n_modules'])}"` matched null modules reaches
`{python} f"{n9['T_null_max']:.3f}"`, and the median null is
`{python} f"{n9['T_null_median']:.3f}"`. Because no null module reaches the observed value, the
module-level permutation probability equals its floor,
`{python} f"{n9['p_module_level']:.6f}"` = 1/`{python} f"{int(n9['n_modules']) + 1}"`. It is a bound,
not an estimate. Co-induction accounts for
`{python} f"{n9['coinduction_share_of_scripts10_effect']:.0%}"` of the effect reported by an
expression-matched null that did not control for induction. The negative control, the false-positive
rate on random modules, is `{python} f"{n9['loo_fpr_module_level']:.3f}"` against a nominal 5%.

One confound is not closed, and the restricted null does not close it. The nine module genes are
co-regulated, and across the null modules the mean gene-gene correlation predicts the statistic
(Spearman `{python} f"{n9['spearman_rhobar_vs_T_null']:+.3f}"`,
p = `{python} f"{n9['spearman_rhobar_vs_T_null_p']:.4f}"`). The observed module's mean correlation,
`{python} f"{n9['rho_bar_real']:.4f}"`, exceeds that of **every** null module (largest
`{python} f"{n9['rho_bar_null_max']:.4f}"`). Restricting the null pool to its most co-regulated
quartile leaves 50 modules, the observed module still exceeds all of them, and the resulting
probability is again the floor,
`{python} f"{n9['p_module_level_coregulated_quartile']:.4f}"` = 1/51. Restriction to a quartile
therefore does not make the observed module exchangeable with the null: it remains more internally
correlated than any module the null can supply. The correct reading is that this confound is open, not
that it has been controlled and the evidence merely attenuated.

## Correlation between the efficacy and co-inhibitory axes {#sec-correlation}

The two axes are not independent. Across all `{python} f"{N_RANKABLE:,}"` perturbations, Spearman
correlation between the efficacy score and co-inhibitory suppression is
`{python} f"{RHO_EFF_TOL:+.3f}"`, rising to `{python} f"{RHO_EFF_TOL_EV:+.3f}"` among the
`{python} f"{N_EVIDENCE}"` perturbations that clear the evidence floor, or
A previous version of this analysis reported only the correlation with panel-wide effect magnitude
(Spearman 0.07) and did not report the correlation above. We do not interpret the square of a Spearman
coefficient as a proportion of shared variance. @sec-module is the control that addresses the resulting
concern, because it holds the perturbation set fixed and varies the module.

## Selection effects on essentiality and immune-deficiency annotations {#sec-selection}

Two annotation-based axes were evaluated as candidate safety filters and both were rejected.

Cancer-cell essentiality cannot be assessed in this screen, because essentiality determines whether a
perturbation enters the analysable set. Of the core-essential genes in the sgRNA library, only a
minority reach the differential expression table, since their knockdown removes the cell; a subsequent
on-target significance filter then removes most nonessential controls. Among the survivors of this
double selection, `{python} f"{int(essent['n_rankable_essentials'])}"` rankable core-essential genes
have a median naive rank of `{python} f"{essent['median_rank_essentials']:.0f}"` against
`{python} f"{essent['median_rank_others']:.0f}"` for the remainder (Mann-Whitney two-sided
p = `{python} f"{essent['mwu_p_two_sided']:.4f}"`). A previously reported figure of 31 rankable
essentials at median rank 3,148 with p = 0.611 is withdrawn; it derived from a gene-symbol vocabulary
error. Neither the withdrawn value nor its replacement supports a causal inference, because the
comparison is made among the survivors of a selection on the exposure.

Immune-essentiality, taken from a curated inborn-errors-of-immunity list, is more enriched among
approved immunomodulator drug targets than among the highest-ranked perturbations. It is therefore
reported as an annotation and never used as a filter.

## Recovery of approved immunomodulator targets {#sec-recovery}

Of `{python} f"{N_POS}"` curated approved-immunomodulator targets, `{python} f"{N_POS_VISIBLE}"` are
quality-control eligible (@sec-qc). The remainder are excluded by the assay or by the mask: the
stimulation carries no polarising cytokines, so cytokine-signalling targets rank near the bottom, and
two targets of an approved JAK inhibitor were never perturbed. Recall is bounded by the assay and the
mask, not by the ranking.

The positive set is not a set of independent genes. It contains correlated mechanism units, including
three calcineurin subunits, two IMPDH paralogues, two IKZF paralogues and three CD3 chains. A
gene-level permutation treats each as an independent recovery and therefore overstates the evidence.
Collapsing the positives into drug-mechanism units is not done here and is a limitation
(@sec-limitations).

```{python}
#| label: fig-recovery
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Recovery of approved immunomodulator targets among the 20 assay-visible curated positives. Observed counts against the expectation under a 50,000-draw permutation null over the screened-gene background. The efficacy axis recovers more positives than chance. The safety axes, evaluated conditional on the evidence floor, add nothing beyond it, which is the expected behaviour of a safety criterion. Permutation probabilities are printed above each pair; the third comparison has no permutation probability because it is conditional and is reported by its hypergeometric probability."
#| fig-width: 7.6
#| fig-height: 3.8

rows = ["positives pass evidence floor", "positives pass full gate", "safety enriches given evidence"]
short = ["efficacy axis\n(evidence floor)", "full triage layer", "safety axes given\nevidence floor"]
obs = [float(recovery.loc[r, "observed"]) for r in rows]
exp = [float(recovery.loc[r, "expected"]) for r in rows]
pv = [recovery.loc[r, "perm_p"] for r in rows]
hp = [recovery.loc[r, "hyper_p"] for r in rows]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.6, 3.8))
axis_style(ax)
x = np.arange(3)
ax.bar(x - 0.19, obs, width=0.36, color=PALETTE["blue"], zorder=3, label="observed")
ax.bar(x + 0.19, exp, width=0.36, color=PALETTE["grey"], zorder=3, label="expected under the null")
for i in range(3):
    label = f"P = {pv[i]:.4f}" if pd.notna(pv[i]) else f"P = {hp[i]:.2f}"
    ax.text(i, max(obs[i], exp[i]) + 0.18, label, ha="center", fontsize=8.5, color=PALETTE["ink"])
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(short, fontsize=8.5)
ax.set_ylabel("curated positives recovered (of 20)", fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylim(0, 6.4)
ax.legend(frameon=False, fontsize=8, loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The efficacy axis recovers `{python} f"{int(recovery.loc['positives pass evidence floor', 'observed'])}"`
of the `{python} f"{N_POS_VISIBLE}"` assay-visible positives against
`{python} f"{recovery.loc['positives pass evidence floor', 'expected']:.2f}"` expected
(permutation p = `{python} f"{recovery.loc['positives pass evidence floor', 'perm_p']:.4f}"`, 50,000
draws). The full triage layer recovers
`{python} f"{int(recovery.loc['positives pass full gate', 'observed'])}"`
(p = `{python} f"{recovery.loc['positives pass full gate', 'perm_p']:.4f}"`). Conditional on the
evidence floor, the safety axes recover
`{python} f"{int(recovery.loc['safety enriches given evidence', 'observed'])}"` against
`{python} f"{recovery.loc['safety enriches given evidence', 'expected']:.2f}"` expected
(p = `{python} f"{recovery.loc['safety enriches given evidence', 'hyper_p']:.2f}"`). Recovery is
therefore attributable to the evidence floor. We do not read the absence of enrichment as evidence of
safety. Approved status is not a safety label, and a null result on a three-gene contingency is not
evidence of anything. This comparison provides limited support for the binary evidence floor and none
for the co-inhibitory criterion.

Rank discrimination on the same positives is absent. Computed on the primary efficacy statistic, the
area under the receiver operating characteristic curve is
`{python} f"{auroc.loc['curated approved-immunomodulator targets', 'auroc']:.3f}"` with a 95% bootstrap
interval of [`{python} f"{auroc.loc['curated approved-immunomodulator targets', 'ci_low']:.3f}"`,
`{python} f"{auroc.loc['curated approved-immunomodulator targets', 'ci_high']:.3f}"`], which contains
chance. Out-of-sample validation of the efficacy axis is instead provided by the held-out screen of
@schmidt2022, where it attains
`{python} f"{auroc.loc['Schmidt 2022 IL-2-reducing hits (HELD OUT)', 'auroc']:.3f}"`
[`{python} f"{auroc.loc['Schmidt 2022 IL-2-reducing hits (HELD OUT)', 'ci_low']:.3f}"`,
`{python} f"{auroc.loc['Schmidt 2022 IL-2-reducing hits (HELD OUT)', 'ci_high']:.3f}"`] against
`{python} f"{int(auroc.loc['Schmidt 2022 IL-2-reducing hits (HELD OUT)', 'n_positives'])}"`
IL-2-reducing hits. Schmidt's hits are the receptor-proximal signalosome that the triage layer rejects,
so a triage score that beat the efficacy axis on this set would be suspect rather than reassuring.

Both curves were previously reported as 0.542 [0.373, 0.707] and 0.702 [0.591, 0.814]. Those values were
computed on a third score, distinct from the primary statistic and from the one used elsewhere. They are
withdrawn.

A drug-recovery benchmark constructed from Open Targets [@ochoa2023] cannot be powered on this dataset.
Against a threshold of 60 positives fixed before counting, the primary count is 38 and the most
permissive upper bound is `{python} f"{int(ceiling['count3_rankable'].max())}"`. This is a property of
the perturbation library rather than of the ranking. An initial count of 173 was inflated by three
annotation artifacts: haematological malignancies classified under immune system disorder;
mechanism-of-action records naming every subunit of a protein complex; and a maximum clinical stage
computed across all indications rather than per indication.

## Mechanistic specificity by gene set enrichment {#sec-gsea}

The efficacy axis could suppress an activation program specifically, or it could move many genes. These
are distinguished by comparing the highest-efficacy perturbations against a set matched on panel-wide
effect magnitude.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gsea
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Preranked gene set enrichment on the MSigDB Hallmark collection, computed on the 48 h stimulated panel-wide $z$ statistic. Top-25 efficacy screen-passing perturbations against a control matched on panel-wide effect magnitude (mean $|z_{L2}|$ 152.7 versus 154.3). Vermillion marks a sign inversion at a false discovery rate below 0.10: negative in the top-efficacy set where the equal-magnitude control is positive. Myc targets, mTORC1 signalling and oxidative phosphorylation are suppressed in both groups and are therefore not evidence of specificity. Both interferon response signatures are induced in both groups, a non-specific stress signature. Inflammatory response inverts but does not clear the false discovery threshold."
#| fig-width: 9.6
#| fig-height: 4.6

top = gsea[gsea["group"] == "top_efficacy"].set_index("Term")
ctl = gsea[gsea["group"] == "magnitude_control"].set_index("Term")
HALL = ["IL-2/STAT5 Signaling", "TNF-alpha Signaling via NF-kB", "Inflammatory Response",
        "Interferon Alpha Response", "Interferon Gamma Response",
        "Myc Targets V1", "mTORC1 Signaling", "Oxidative Phosphorylation"]
missing = set(HALL) - set(top.index)
if missing:
    raise KeyError(f"gsea_hallmark.csv is missing {missing}")

nes_t = np.array([top.loc[t, "NES"] for t in HALL])
nes_c = np.array([ctl.loc[t, "NES"] for t in HALL])
fdr_t = np.array([top.loc[t, "FDR q-val"] for t in HALL])
flip, sig = (nes_t < 0) & (nes_c > 0), fdr_t < 0.10
y = np.arange(len(HALL))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9.6, 4.6))
axis_style(ax, xgrid=True, ygrid=False)
ax.axvline(0, color=PALETTE["axis"], linewidth=1)
ax.barh(y + 0.19, nes_c, height=0.34, color=PALETTE["grey"], edgecolor="white", zorder=3)
ax.barh(y - 0.19, nes_t, height=0.34, edgecolor="white", zorder=3,
        color=[PALETTE["vermillion"] if (f and s) else PALETTE["blue"] for f, s in zip(flip, sig)])
for i in np.where(flip)[0]:
    note = f"FDR {fdr_t[i]:.3f}" + ("" if sig[i] else " (n.s.)")
    ax.text(nes_t[i] - 0.09, i - 0.19, note, ha="right", va="center", fontsize=7.5,
            color=PALETTE["ink"] if sig[i] else PALETTE["muted"])
ax.set_yticks(y)
ax.set_yticklabels(HALL, fontsize=8.5)
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_xlabel("normalised enrichment score", fontsize=9)
ax.legend(handles=[mpl.patches.Patch(facecolor=PALETTE["grey"], label="magnitude-matched control"),
                   mpl.patches.Patch(facecolor=PALETTE["blue"], label="top efficacy, same direction"),
                   mpl.patches.Patch(facecolor=PALETTE["vermillion"], label="top efficacy, sign inversion, FDR < 0.10")],
          loc="lower right", frameon=False, fontsize=7.5)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="The enrichment test is partly circular"}
Eleven of the 34 effector-module genes that define the efficacy score are members of the tested
`IL-2/STAT5 Signaling` set, and eleven are members of `TNF-alpha Signaling via NF-kB`. Those genes
remain in the ranked lists. The reported false discovery rates also test enrichment within each
aggregated ranked list separately; they do not test whether enrichment differs between the top-efficacy
group and the matched control. A non-circular version would remove the score-defining genes from both
the ranked lists and the tested sets, use repeated matched controls, and test the difference in
enrichment directly by permutation over perturbations. That analysis is not reported here
(@sec-limitations).
:::

IL-2/STAT5 signalling reaches a normalised enrichment score of
`{python} f"{top.loc['IL-2/STAT5 Signaling', 'NES']:.3f}"`
(FDR `{python} f"{top.loc['IL-2/STAT5 Signaling', 'FDR q-val']:.3f}"`) in the top-efficacy set and
`{python} f"{ctl.loc['IL-2/STAT5 Signaling', 'NES']:+.3f}"` in the magnitude-matched control. TNF-alpha
signalling via NF-kB reaches `{python} f"{top.loc['TNF-alpha Signaling via NF-kB', 'NES']:.3f}"` against
`{python} f"{ctl.loc['TNF-alpha Signaling via NF-kB', 'NES']:+.3f}"`. Myc targets are suppressed to
`{python} f"{top.loc['Myc Targets V1', 'NES']:.2f}"` and
`{python} f"{ctl.loc['Myc Targets V1', 'NES']:.2f}"` respectively, indistinguishably. The specificity is
an inversion of the immune signatures at equal effect magnitude, not a larger effect.

## Sensitivity of the nomination rule on recovered drug targets {#sec-nomination}

The pipeline recovers `{python} f"{N_RECOVERED}"` approved-drug targets among the
`{python} f"{N_SAFE}"` screen-passing genes. Applying a candidate nomination rule to those targets, and
asking how many it would re-nominate if their drug status were unknown, calibrates the rule without
reference to any novel gene.

```{python}
tbl = sens.reset_index()[["rule", "recovered_drugs", "novel_nominated"]].copy()
tbl.columns = ["rule", "recovered drug targets re-nominated (of 6)", "novel genes returned"]
Markdown(tbl.to_markdown(index=False))
```

A rule requiring autoimmune genetic support, loss-of-function tolerance and tractability simultaneously
re-nominates `{python} f"{int(sens.loc['supported & lof_tolerant & tractable', 'recovered_drugs'])}"` of
the six and returns
`{python} f"{int(sens.loc['supported & lof_tolerant & tractable', 'novel_nominated'])}"` novel gene. At
n = 6 that proportion has an exact 95% confidence interval of [0.12, 0.88] and does not by itself
reject the rule (one-sided binomial P = 0.062 against a 5/6 criterion). The rule is rejected on two
other grounds. It returns one gene from 206 candidates. And its failures are deterministic properties
of database coverage rather than sampling: the genetic-support requirement removes the targets of
anti-CD3, mycophenolate and ciclosporin, each of which has an Open Targets autoimmune genetic score of
exactly 0.000, and the loss-of-function tolerance requirement removes the target of mycophenolate,
whose pRec is 0.999. These are the essential-hub mechanisms that phenotypic pharmacology has repeatedly
found. Loss-of-function tolerance in humans is a constraint on a complete knockout [@minikel2020];
mycophenolate is an approved reversible inhibitor of a gene that is intolerant of biallelic loss, so the
criterion bears on the therapeutic modality and not on whether the gene can be a target.

Selecting instead the most restrictive rule whose positive-class sensitivity is complete gives the
screen gate combined with structural tractability and the exclusion of direction-discordant genes. It
returns `{python} f"{N_NOMINATED}"` candidates.

::: {.callout-important title="A pre-registered control on this rule fails"}
```{python}
ctrl = pd.read_csv(T / "nomination_controls.csv").set_index("control")
p_obs = float(ctrl.loc["recovery, conditioned on the safety gate", "p_value"])
p_ceil = float(ctrl.loc["power ceiling: all safe-set positives retained", "p_value"])
n_obs = int(ctrl.loc["recovery, conditioned on the safety gate", "observed"])
e_obs = float(ctrl.loc["recovery, conditioned on the safety gate", "expected"])
Markdown(
    f"The rule was required to preserve recovery of curated positives under a permutation null that "
    f"holds the screen gate fixed. It recovers {n_obs} positives against {e_obs:.2f} expected, "
    f"p = {p_obs:.3f}. Only {int(den['positives among screen-passing genes'])} curated positives lie "
    f"within the screen-passing set, so a rule retaining all of them could not attain a probability "
    f"below {p_ceil:.3f}. **The control fails, and the rebuilt rule is void by its own criterion.** "
    f"`scripts/29_nomination_recalibration.py` exits with a non-zero status. The "
    f"{N_NOMINATED}-candidate pool is reported for completeness and supports no nomination."
)
```
:::

The rule was rebuilt after the initial result was known. It is exploratory model repair, transparently
logged, and its controls are sanity checks rather than confirmatory statistics. One of those checks now
fails.

Clinical precedent, meaning that a drug against the gene has reached the clinic, is reported and never
used as a criterion. It is true of 32 of the 36 curated positives, so a rule reading it would recover
approved drug targets by construction.

## Availability of direction-of-effect annotation {#sec-direction}

Because CRISPRi models an inhibitor, a nomination requires that loss of function be the therapeutic
direction. These are **manually assigned annotations, not adjudicated measurements**. An annotation was
available for `{python} f"{N_ADJUDICABLE}"` of the `{python} f"{N_SAFE}"` screen-passing genes;
`{python} f"{N_UNMEASURED}"` have none.

```{python}
b = doe["direction_basis"].value_counts().reset_index()
b.columns = ["basis", "genes"]
Markdown(b.to_markdown(index=False))
```

Two earlier rules were withdrawn. The set of action types treated as loss-of-function-mimicking
included `BINDING AGENT` and `CROSS-LINKING AGENT`; neither implies reduced target activity, and the
anti-CD3 and anti-CD28 reagents that stimulate the cells in this screen are cross-linking agonists.
A second rule inferred concordance from an inborn error of immunity, on the reasoning that a gene whose
loss impairs immunity must promote it. That inference is unsafe. `PTEN` is an inborn-error gene whose
loss dysregulates PI3K signalling and produces lymphoid hyperplasia and autoimmunity, and the rule
called it concordant. Immunodeficiency and autoimmunity are not opposite ends of one axis. `PTEN` is
now unannotated.

What remains is asymmetric. Every concordant annotation requires an approved drug whose mechanism
mimics loss of function, so the conditional probability of a concordant annotation given a gene with no
approved drug is zero and the missingness is not at random. The two discordant annotations come from a
hand-curated list of canonical negative regulators, which is a judgement rather than a measurement.
Direction can exclude a candidate; it cannot promote one. Excluding the two discordant genes licenses
no direction-safety claim about the remaining `{python} f"{N_UNMEASURED}"`.

`PTPN2` illustrates the consequence. It has strong autoimmune genetic support [@wtccc2007] and would be
promoted by a genetics-weighted ranking, but it is a negative regulator of T cell receptor signalling
whose loss causes autoimmunity in mice [@wiede2011] and, in patients with germline heterozygous
loss-of-function variants, early-onset systemic autoimmunity [@jeanpierre2024]. A knockdown screen
models the wrong direction for this gene. Human genetic support is direction-agnostic and is a prior on
success rather than a sufficient condition [@nelson2015; @king2019; @minikel2024].

Attempts to obtain direction from data that are not characterisation-bound failed for reasons specific
to each instrument. Colocalisation of autoimmune GWAS signals with expression quantitative trait loci
was calibrated on a nine-gene panel of known direction and gated to apply only at an accuracy of 0.80;
it attained 0.50 and was not applied. The reason is principled: an expression QTL measures transcript
abundance, whereas the protective `TYK2` P1104A allele is a coding hypomorph with near-normal
expression [@dendrou2016].

## External replication in an independent primary human CD4 screen {#sec-freimer}

@freimer2022 provides signed, protein-level readouts in primary human CD4 T cells for IL-2, the
activation output that the efficacy axis scores, and for CTLA-4, one of the nine genes in the
co-inhibitory module. It is a **targeted** screen of approximately 1,350 transcription factors and
chromatin modifiers, not a genome-wide replication cohort, and it shares that library exactly with
@arce2025 (Jaccard index 1.0000). We therefore describe the comparison below as orthogonal
concordance rather than replication.

```{python}
t = ov[["hypothesis", "n", "rho", "stratified_on", "p_matched", "verdict"]].copy()
t["rho"] = t["rho"].map(lambda r: f"{r:+.3f}")
t["p_matched"] = t["p_matched"].map(lambda p: f"{p:.4f}")
t.columns = ["hypothesis", "n", "Spearman", "permutation null stratified on", "P", "verdict"]
Markdown(t.to_markdown(index=False))
```

The efficacy axis is associated with IL-2 loss (Spearman `{python} f"{H2['rho'].iloc[0]:+.3f}"`,
n = `{python} f"{int(H2['n'].iloc[0])}"`), and the association persists after coarse stratification on
all four covariates, including resting-arm transcriptional disruption. The effect is small. The
co-inhibitory axis is not associated with CTLA-4 loss (Spearman
`{python} f"{H1['rho'].iloc[0]:+.3f}"`) under any stratification.

```{python}
#| label: fig-freimer
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "External comparison with the independent primary human CD4 CRISPR screen of Freimer et al. **A.** Genes whose knockout significantly lowers IL-2 carry far more resting-arm differential expression than genes that do not, so a thresholded IL-2 hit call partly reports impaired transcription of an induced gene rather than specific immunosuppression. **B.** The continuous association between the mRNA efficacy score and the signed protein-level IL-2 effect survives a permutation null stratified jointly on panel-wide effect magnitude and resting-arm differential expression. The continuous axis replicates; the thresholded hit call, which a promotion rule would use, does not. Grey, non-hits; vermillion, IL-2-lowering hits."
#| fig-width: 9.6
#| fig-height: 4.2

fh = fdata[fdata["freimer_il2_lowers"]]
fn = fdata[~fdata["freimer_il2_lowers"]]
p_joint = float(rev["h2_p_stratified_jointly"])

fig, (axa, axb) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.6, 4.2))

axis_style(axa)
bp = axa.boxplot([np.log10(fn["rest_de_genes"] + 1), np.log10(fh["rest_de_genes"] + 1)],
                 patch_artist=True, widths=0.5, showfliers=False, zorder=3)
for patch, colour in zip(bp["boxes"], [PALETTE["grey"], PALETTE["vermillion"]]):
    patch.set_facecolor(colour)
    patch.set_edgecolor(PALETTE["axis"])
for part in ("whiskers", "caps", "medians"):
    for element in bp[part]:
        element.set_color(PALETTE["ink2"])
axa.set_xticklabels([f"not an IL-2 hit\n(n = {len(fn)})", f"IL-2-lowering hit\n(n = {len(fh)})"], fontsize=8.5)
axa.set_ylabel("resting-arm DE genes, $\\log_{10}(x+1)$", fontsize=9)
axa.text(0.03, 0.96, f"median {fn['rest_de_genes'].median():.0f} vs {fh['rest_de_genes'].median():.0f}\n"
                     f"Mann-Whitney P = {rev['mwu_p_hits_more_resting_disruption']:.1e}",
         transform=axa.transAxes, fontsize=8, color=PALETTE["ink2"], va="top")
panel_label(axa, "A")

axis_style(axb)
axb.scatter(fn["efficacy"], fn["freimer_il2_lfc"], s=10, color=PALETTE["grey"], zorder=2, linewidths=0)
axb.scatter(fh["efficacy"], fh["freimer_il2_lfc"], s=22, color=PALETTE["vermillion"], zorder=3, linewidths=0)
axb.axhline(0, color=PALETTE["axis"], linewidth=0.9)
axb.set_xlabel("efficacy score (mRNA)", fontsize=9)
axb.set_ylabel("Freimer IL-2 effect (protein)\nhigher = knockout lowers IL-2", fontsize=9)
axb.text(0.03, 0.96, f"Spearman = {float(H2['rho'].iloc[0]):+.3f}, n = {len(fdata)}\n"
                     f"P = {p_joint:.4f}, stratified jointly on\npanel-wide magnitude and resting DE",
         transform=axb.transAxes, fontsize=8, color=PALETTE["ink2"], va="top")
panel_label(axb, "B")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The thresholded hit call is confounded. The `{python} f"{int(rev['n_hits'])}"` genes called
IL-2-lowering carry a median of `{python} f"{rev['median_rest_de_hits']:.0f}"` resting-arm
differentially expressed genes, against `{python} f"{rev['median_rest_de_nonhits']:.0f}"` for the
`{python} f"{int(rev['n_nonhits'])}"` remaining genes (Mann-Whitney
P = `{python} f"{rev['mwu_p_hits_more_resting_disruption']:.1e}"`). IL-2 is a highly induced transcript,
so a perturbation that impairs transcription broadly will lower it. The continuous association persists after coarse
stratification on that confound; the hit call does not, and a promotion rule uses the hit call.

## Exploratory relaxation of the promotion rule {#sec-relaxation}

Restricted to the `{python} f"{N_NOMINATED}"`-gene nomination pool and to a pre-declared coverage
requirement of ten co-tested genes, no candidate could be assessed: only six pool genes are co-tested in
the IL-2 arm. A subsequent exploratory relaxation removed the coverage requirement and promoted genes
whose knockout significantly lowers IL-2. It returned two genes, `{python} RELAXED_GENES`.

Both fail criteria specified earlier in this manuscript. Measured in this screen, `ATXN7L3` lies at the
96.2nd percentile of resting-arm differential expression and has a stimulated-over-resting ratio of
0.92, against the pre-specified tenfold context-selectivity criterion; a ratio below one means the
perturbation disturbs the unstimulated cell more than the stimulated cell. `XBP1` lies at the 92.1st
percentile. Neither carries autoimmune genetic support, a small-molecule binding pocket, or clinical
precedent. Neither shows CTLA-4 lowering, which is the absence of a liability signal and not evidence
of preservation.

The comparator library is a panel of transcription factors and chromatin modifiers, so the co-tested
gene set is enriched for transcriptional and chromatin machinery by construction. Both promoted genes
are drawn from that enrichment, and neither result should be read as a statement about T cell biology.

# Discussion

We built a triage layer for a genome-scale CD4+ T cell Perturb-seq atlas from quantities the screen
measures, and we report the conditions under which it declines to nominate.

Three of its properties are supported. Ranking by effector-program suppression returns perturbations
that an independent protein-level screen confirms are effective and that this layer refuses, and the
refusal is specific to the co-inhibitory axis rather than to effect magnitude. The efficacy axis
suppresses IL-2/STAT5 and TNF-alpha/NF-kB signatures where an equal-magnitude control does not, and it
replicates on an independent, protein-level, primary human CD4 screen under four stratified nulls. It
recovers approved immunomodulator targets above chance among the assay-visible positives, while the
safety axes add nothing beyond it, which is the behaviour a safety criterion should show.

Several properties are not supported, and we state them before any conclusion that rests on the layer.

The co-inhibitory module is an interpretation. In a 48 h stimulation of bulk primary human conventional
CD4 T cells, its receptors are activation-induced, and FOXP3 is transiently induced without conferring
suppressive function [@wang2007; @tran2007; @allan2007]. We measured messenger RNA. We did not measure
tolerance, regulatory identity, suppression, or a clinical outcome.

The two triage axes share approximately `{python} f"{RHO_EFF_TOL_EV**2:.0%}"` of rank variance among
evidence-passing perturbations. The module-level control addresses this by holding the perturbation set
fixed, and the module remains more suppressed than expression- and induction-matched modules. That
evidence attenuates when the null pool is restricted to comparably co-regulated modules, and we report
the attenuated probability. Independently, the co-inhibitory axis does not predict CTLA-4 protein loss
in the comparator screen under any of four nulls, whereas the efficacy axis predicts IL-2 loss under
all four. The co-inhibitory axis is therefore internally validated and externally uncorroborated at
protein level for its best-measured member. CTLA-4 is one of nine module genes, the comparator measures
surface protein in a different stimulation context, and only 13 CTLA-4-lowering hits are available, so
the negative is bounded; but the same contrast detects the efficacy signal at 29 hits, so it is not
merely insensitive.

The binding constraint on nomination is not the ranking method. It is the absence of a direction-of-
effect measurement for `{python} f"{N_UNMEASURED}"` of the `{python} f"{N_SAFE}"` screen-passing genes,
and the fact that the annotations which do carry direction are available only for genes that a drug or
a monogenic syndrome has already characterised. Direction can exclude; it cannot promote. Instruments
that are not characterisation-bound failed for reasons intrinsic to each: an expression QTL reports
abundance rather than activity [@dendrou2016]; and the comparator screens, although functional and
signed, use a targeted transcription-factor and chromatin-modifier library [@freimer2022; @arce2025]
whose intersection with a genome-scale screen is small and non-random.

Recall is bounded by the assay. Stimulation through the T cell receptor without polarising cytokines
places cytokine-signalling targets near the bottom of the ranking, and single-gene CRISPRi cannot see a
redundant target: the two catalytic calcineurin paralogues each yield fewer than four differentially
expressed genes, whereas the non-redundant regulatory subunit yields 523. A drug-recovery benchmark
built on this dataset is underpowered by a factor of roughly two against a threshold fixed in advance,
and this is a property of the perturbation library rather than of the method.

Finally, selection determines what can be asked. Essentiality cannot be evaluated as a safety axis in a
screen where essentiality governs entry into the analysable set, and the comparison among survivors of
that selection supports no causal inference in either direction. A nomination rule that conjoins
external annotations re-nominates only three of the six approved-drug targets the same pipeline
recovers, because two of those annotations are absent for the essential-hub mechanisms that phenotypic
pharmacology has historically produced.

No gene met the pre-specified nomination standard, and the pre-registered control on the rebuilt
nomination rule fails for want of power. We regard abstention as the correct output of the layer given
the available evidence, rather than as a limitation of the screen. Resolving it requires an instrument
that is signed, functional, primary-human-CD4, and genome-wide. None of the three we evaluated
satisfies all four conditions simultaneously.

## Limitations {#sec-limitations}

The work is an exploratory computational reanalysis. It is not a validated method for target discovery,
safety assessment, or therapeutic-window estimation, and the following are known deficiencies rather
than hypothetical ones.

**The co-inhibitory criterion is a transcript annotation.** Its nine genes are not one molecular
mechanism, two of them fail the induction criterion applied elsewhere, and messenger RNA abundance is
not functional checkpoint availability. Its only external, protein-level comparison is negative. The
observed module is more internally correlated than every module the matched null can supply, so
restricting the null to co-regulated modules does not make it exchangeable. Separate checkpoint,
cytokine and regulatory-lineage components, per-gene and leave-one-gene-out analyses, covariance-matched
coherent modules, and measurement of surface CTLA-4, PD-1, LAG-3 and TIGIT with a functional readout are
all required before this can be called a preservation axis. None is reported here.

**The 197-gene set is partly definitional.** The co-inhibitory cut is a quantile of the evidence-passing
set, so it rejects one quarter of that set whatever the distribution. Threshold sensitivity, rank
stability, and uncertainty in set membership are not reported, and the cut is not calibrated against any
external adverse phenotype.

**Donor and guide uncertainty is not propagated.** The primary results use pooled effects across four
donors and generally two guides per target. We report no leave-one-donor-out stability, no guide
concordance, no confidence interval on any gene score, and no stability analysis for the
`{python} f"{N_SAFE}"`-member set. This matters concretely: `PPP3R1`, used here as a recovered positive,
has two guides whose signatures anticorrelate on significant genes in the released metadata. Gene-level
claims in this manuscript should be read with that in mind.

**The enrichment analysis is partly circular**, as stated in @sec-gsea, and the recovery analysis treats
correlated mechanism units as independent genes.

**The efficacy score is not disease-specific.** It measures suppression of a polyclonal activation
program. Suppressing IL-2/STAT5 signalling is not uniformly therapeutic: low-dose interleukin-2 is used
clinically to expand regulatory T cells in autoimmune disease. Nothing here identifies an indication.

**The quality-control mask is ours** (@sec-qc), it removes a majority of the perturbations tested in the
stimulated arm, and no sensitivity analysis under relaxed masks is reported.

**The comparator screens are targeted and share one library.** @freimer2022 and @arce2025 assay the same
approximately 1,350 transcription factors and chromatin modifiers. They are independent in readout and
cell state, not in gene space, and the 471 genes co-tested against this screen are not a random sample
of the perturbed genome.

# Data and code availability

The Perturb-seq effect matrices are released by @zhu2025 and are obtained from the public S3 bucket
`s3://genome-scale-tcell-perturb-seq/marson2025_data/`. Open Targets release 26.06 is pinned. All
analysis code, every committed intermediate table, and the source of this document are available at
`https://github.com/wguesdon/cd4-perturbseq-target-discovery`.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

# Supplementary information {.appendix}

## Provenance of every reported statistic {#sec-supp-genealogy}

Each quantity that has been quoted during this analysis, its current status, and the reason it changed.
Values marked *current* are re-read from their source table by `scripts/30_pvalue_genealogy.py`, which
exits non-zero if any has drifted.

```{python}
g = gen[["claim_id", "claim", "previous_value", "current_value", "current_status"]].copy()
g.columns = ["id", "claim", "previously", "current", "status"]
Markdown(g.to_markdown(index=False))
```

## Denominators {#sec-supp-denominators}

```{python}
d = pd.read_csv(T / "denominator_manifest.csv")[["universe", "n", "meaning"]].copy()
d["n"] = d["n"].map(lambda x: f"{x:,}")
Markdown(d.to_markdown(index=False))
```

## Gene sets and candidate lists {#sec-supp-genes}

```{python}
import sys as _sys
_sys.path.insert(0, str(REPO / "src"))
from cd4_perturbseq import programs as _programs

eff_genes = sorted(_programs.effector_core())
tol_genes = sorted(_programs.tolerance_module())
safe_genes = sorted(window.loc[window["safe"], "gene_name"])
Markdown(
    f"**Effector module, {len(eff_genes)} genes.** " + ", ".join(f"`{g}`" for g in eff_genes) + "\n\n"
    f"**Activation-induced co-inhibitory transcript module, {len(tol_genes)} genes.** "
    + ", ".join(f"`{g}`" for g in tol_genes) + "\n\n"
    f"**Screen-passing perturbations, {len(safe_genes)}.** Full table with every axis and annotation: "
    f"`results/tables/window_score.csv` (filter on `safe`). Nomination pool of {N_NOMINATED}: "
    f"`results/tables/nomination_recalibrated.csv`."
)
```

## Withdrawn claims and corrections {#sec-supp-withdrawn}

The abstract refers to this list. It is not fixed in length, and it grew during peer review.

**Corrections made in response to peer review.**

- The manuscript used two different efficacy statistics: a z score for ranking and a mean log fold
  change for gating, gene-set selection, recovery and external comparison. Those orderings share 9 of
  their top 20 and 49 of their top 100 (Spearman 0.866). A single signed z-based statistic is now used
  throughout.
- The evidence floor counted significantly decreased module genes without requiring net suppression. It
  admitted five perturbations that induce the effector module, one of them the curated positive `IL4R`
  (efficacy −0.086 on the log-fold-change scale). A direction requirement was added; evidence passers
  fell from 286 to `{python} f"{N_EVIDENCE}"` and screen-passing genes from 214 to
  `{python} f"{N_SAFE}"`.
- Monte Carlo probabilities were computed without the `+1` correction, and `P < 0.0001` was reported
  from 5,000 draws. Corrected to the floor `{python} f"{ANY['p_matched_ge_observed']:.1e}"` = 1/5001.
- Module-level probabilities of 1/201 and 1/51 were described as an estimate and as attenuated
  evidence. Both are floors, and the restricted null does not make the observed module exchangeable.
- The action types treated as loss-of-function-mimicking included `BINDING AGENT` and `CROSS-LINKING
  AGENT`. Both were removed. A rule inferring concordance from an inborn error of immunity was
  withdrawn; it called `PTEN` concordant.
- The rankable set was attributed to the source authors' quality control. It is our own mask (@sec-qc).
- @arce2025 was described as a fitness screen. It is a FACS marker screen on IL-2Rα surface expression,
  and it shares its library exactly with @freimer2022.

**Claims withdrawn earlier in the analysis.**

1. A per-perturbation Mann-Whitney probability of 3.5e-13 for co-inhibitory module suppression treats
   `{python} f"{N_RANKABLE:,}"` z statistics as independent when the module is a single fixed nine-gene
   set. Its false-positive rate on random modules was
   `{python} f"{n9['mwu_fpr_per_perturbation']:.0%}"` against a nominal 5%. It is superseded by the
   module-level permutation probability in @sec-module.
2. A single sampled matched background made the reported probability a random variable; 22.4% of seeds
   returned a non-significant result. It is superseded by stratified tests over every control row.
3. A drug-recovery benchmark count of 173 approved immune targets was inflated by three annotation
   artifacts and is superseded by a primary count of 38.
4. An essentiality comparison reporting 31 rankable core-essential genes at median rank 3,148 with
   p = 0.611 derived from a gene-symbol vocabulary error. It is superseded by
   `{python} f"{int(essent['n_rankable_essentials'])}"` rankable genes at median rank
   `{python} f"{essent['median_rank_essentials']:.0f}"`, and neither value supports a causal inference
   (@sec-selection).
5. Tractability was briefly defined to include clinical precedent. Since clinical precedent is true of
   32 of the 36 curated positives, a nomination rule reading it would recover approved drug targets by
   construction. The definition was reverted and the criterion is reported, not gated on.
